one_line_takeaway: By forging many position-hypothesis seals and adaptively picking the best one per generation step instead of statically blending a fixed set, Adaptive Stealing pushes scrubbing detectability of KGW, SynthID, and Unbiased watermarks down to near-random AUC (<0.55) using only 10,000 victim samples, substantially outperforming the prior state-of-the-art stealing attack WS.
key_finding: AS strictly dominates WS across every watermark, victim model, and dataset tested. Scrubbing (Table 1): AS drives AUC below 0.55 in all 3 watermarks x 2 models x 2 datasets combinations (best case KGW/OPT-2.7B/Dolly: AUC 0.47, TPR@1%=0.03, down from Dipper-only AUC 0.75/TPR 0.05 and Dipper+WS AUC 0.69/TPR 0.08), whereas the lowest AUC WS ever achieves is 0.55 -- i.e. AS pushes detectability to near the random-guess floor (0.50) while WS does not. Spoofing (Table 2): AS improves AUC over WS by 0.04-0.17 across settings, e.g. on KGW/OPT-2.7B/Dolly, w/o-attack AUC=1.00 (WCS 8.880), WS achieves AUC 0.74 (WCS 1.228, TPR@1%=0.06), AS achieves AUC 0.91 (WCS 2.698, TPR@1%=0.36); on Unbiased/Llama3-8B/Harm, WS AUC 0.71 vs AS AUC 0.78. Data efficiency: AS needs only 2,000 victim watermarked samples to match the spoofing WCS that WS achieves with 10,000 (Figure 4). Ablation (Table 3, KGW/Llama3-8B/Dolly): a single matched seal (no=1, leftmost-token activation) achieves AUC=0.997 when the true victim scheme is LeftHash with |ctx|=1, but degrades sharply on mismatched configurations (e.g. that same seal drops to AUC~0.50-0.56 on |ctx|=4); the naive equal-weight ensemble (AVE) gives only limited gains over single seals, while full Adaptive Selection under the realistic 'Unknown' setting (true scheme/context length unknown to attacker) reaches AUC=0.843, beating both single-seal and AVE baselines. Text-quality trade-off: at matched perplexity, AS maintains higher AUC than WS, particularly in the high-detectability regime (AUC>0.75), indicating the gain comes from better information use rather than sacrificing fluency.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Adaptive Stealing (AS), a new stealing-watermark algorithm (SWA) for LLM text watermarks that replaces prior SWAs' single fixed 'seal' with two novel modules: (1) Position-Based Seal Construction, which systematically enumerates 2^|ctx| 'ordered transformations' of the context window (each corresponding to a binary activation pattern over context token positions) to forge a diverse set of candidate seals that hypothesize different ways a victim watermark's Hash-scheme might process context tokens; and (2) Adaptive Selection, which at each generation step picks the single best candidate seal via a scoring function omega(no, ctx) combining three criteria -- Dynamic Generation Relevance (restrict to the current top-k highest-probability tokens under the attacker LM), Watermark Compatibility (weight by the empirical estimate of the victim's true conditional distribution p_hat_w(T|ctx)), and Generation Priority (the normalized impression score of that seal for T). The selected seal's impression is then applied via logits modification (l_hat = l + delta_att * im_hat, with delta_att sign flipped for scrubbing vs. spoofing) exactly as in the prior baseline WS (Jovanovic et al. 2024). The paper frames AS as an attack-evaluation tool ('understanding attacks to build better defenses') rather than an attack-enabling release, though it also releases code publicly.
threat_model: Attacker has: (1) no knowledge of the victim watermark's secret key K or hyperparameters; (2) a limited budget of queries to the victim LLM to collect a small set Dw of watermarked texts (as few as 10,000, tested down to 100); (3) unlimited access to non-watermarked text Dn; (4) an assistant/attacker LLM (Qwen2.5-7B in experiments) to generate candidate continuations; and (5) explicitly NO access to the watermark's detection interface (no oracle queries to verify success). Two victim LLMs (OPT-2.7B, Llama3-8B) each equipped with one of three watermarking schemes (KGW/LeftHash, SynthID, Unbiased) are attacked. Two attack goals are evaluated: spoofing (forge watermark signal into non-watermarked/attacker text to falsely implicate the victim model) and scrubbing (remove/paraphrase-and-reinject-resistant watermark signal from genuinely watermarked text to evade detection).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) AS incurs ~1.6x generation latency versus WS due to evaluating multiple candidate seals per step; (2) evaluation covers only two victim models (OPT-2.7B, Llama3-8B) and three watermark schemes (KGW-LeftHash, SynthID, Unbiased) due to time/resource constraints -- notably the high-security SelfHash-scheme KGW variant (WS's original target) was excluded because it is ~30x slower to generate with (>300s vs <10s per 200 tokens on a V100), so AS's advantage there is untested; (3) multilingual attack effectiveness is untested -- experiments are English-only; (4) AS applies the stolen impression via the same logits-modification mechanism as WS, which the authors note causes 'relatively large damage to text quality,' leaving the impression-application step itself unoptimized. Additional grounding-note-worthy caveats not flagged by the authors as limitations but visible in the data: the single baseline compared is WS; older/other SWAs are excluded from the main tables and only discussed qualitatively in an appendix, so the magnitude of AS's advantage over the full landscape of stealing methods is not directly quantified in the main results.

datasets_used: C4 realnews-like subset (source of victim watermarked texts Dw, |Dw|=10,000 default, swept 100-10,000)
Harm prompts (constructed by merging HarmfulQA and AdvBench)
quantitative_results: Scrubbing, KGW/OPT-2.7B/Dolly: Dipper AUC=0.75, Dipper+WS AUC=0.69, Dipper+AS AUC=0.47
Scrubbing, all 3 watermarks x 2 models x 2 datasets: AS AUC always < 0.55; lowest AUC achieved by WS is 0.55
Spoofing, KGW/OPT-2.7B/Dolly: w/o Attack AUC=1.00 (WCS 8.880), WS AUC=0.74 (WCS 1.228, TPR@1%=0.06), AS AUC=0.91 (WCS 2.698, TPR@1%=0.36)
Spoofing overall: AS improves AUC over WS by 0.04-0.17 across watermark/model/dataset combinations
|Dw| efficiency: AS needs only 2,000 victim watermarked samples to match WS's spoofing WCS at 10,000 samples

Ablation (KGW/Llama3-8B/Dolly, Table 3): single matched seal no=1 at |ctx|=1&Left gives AUC=0.997; AVE (equal-weight ensemble of 8 seals) gives limited improvement over single seals; full AS under 'Unknown' scheme/context-length setting achieves AUC=0.843
AS incurs ~1.6x generation latency versus WS (self-reported limitation)
baselines_compared: WS (Watermark Stealing, Jovanovic et al. 2024) -- primary and only baseline run in main Tables 1-2
Random classifier (AUC=0.50, TPR@1%=0.01) as detection floor reference
Dipper-only paraphrasing (no watermark-aware stealing) as a scrubbing lower bound
AVE (equal-weight ensemble of Position-Based Seal Construction's candidate seals, no Adaptive Selection) as an internal ablation baseline

CWS (Sadasivan et al. 2023), WRA (Pan et al. 2025), MIP (Zhang et al. 2024) mentioned as other existing SWAs but described as 'less effective in our scenarios' and relegated to appendix, not included in main quantitative tables
